title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Colour, Fire and Chemistry: The Science of Fireworks
content.text: The crowd falls silent as the first shell climbs into the dark sky. A moment later, the night explodes in cascading gold and crimson. For most spectators, a fireworks display is pure magic. For a pyrotechnician, it is applied chemistry. (37) [37] Every burst of colour and pattern is the result of carefully calculated reactions between specific chemical compounds. [/37] || The colours in fireworks are produced by metal salts that emit particular wavelengths of light when heated. Strontium compounds give red; barium compounds produce green; copper salts create blue; and sodium yields a brilliant yellow-orange. (38) [38] Achieving a true blue has historically been one of the greatest challenges in pyrotechnic chemistry, since the relevant copper compounds are unstable at high temperatures. [/38] || Timing and sequence are controlled by a combination of fuses and electronic firing systems. In large professional displays, hundreds of individual shells may be launched in a precisely choreographed sequence, each timed to within a fraction of a second. (39) [39] The coordination required resembles that of a theatrical production, with the pyrotechnician as both director and stage manager. [/39] || The shells themselves are engineering marvels. A typical aerial shell contains a lifting charge, a delay fuse, a bursting charge and the 'stars' — small pellets of chemical mixture that produce the coloured light. (40) [40] The geometry of the stars' arrangement inside the shell determines the shape of the final burst, whether spherical, cylindrical or a complex pattern such as a heart or a palm tree. [/40] || Safety is the industry's paramount concern. Fireworks manufacturing takes place in isolated facilities, with strict limits on the quantities of material handled at any one time. (41) [41] Even with these precautions, accidents can and do happen, which is why training and certification are mandatory for professional pyrotechnicians. [/41] || The industry is also evolving in response to environmental concerns. Low-smoke and low-noise formulations are being developed for use in residential areas, and some companies are experimenting with electronically ignited displays that produce light without combustion. (42) [42] The goal is to preserve the wonder of fireworks while reducing their impact on air quality and wildlife. [/42]

paragraphs.A: The precise proportions of each ingredient must be measured to within fractions of a gram.
paragraphs.B: Each of these metals was identified through painstaking experimentation over centuries.
paragraphs.C: Recent breakthroughs in stabilising these compounds have expanded the colour palette available to designers.
paragraphs.D: Regulations also govern storage, transport and the minimum distances from audiences.
paragraphs.E: Producing a long display that tells a visual story requires months of planning and rehearsal.
paragraphs.F: The result is a precisely engineered explosion that unfolds exactly as the designer intended.
paragraphs.G: These innovations may define the next chapter in a craft that is already hundreds of years old.

questions.explanation: A mentions that the proportions of each ingredient must be measured precisely, supporting the idea that fireworks are applied chemistry.
C talks about recent advances in stabilising copper compounds, fitting perfectly after mentioning the historical difficulty of obtaining blue.
E explains that a long display that tells a visual story requires months of planning, supporting the comparison with a theatrical production.
F describes the result as a precisely engineered explosion that unfolds exactly as the designer intended, summarising the rocket's engineering.
D mentions regulations on storage and minimum distances, adding further safety precautions to the corresponding paragraph.
G suggests that these innovations will define the next chapter of the craft, closing the article with a look towards the future.
